text: Reviewing papers for new journals is becoming a growing challenge for journal editors and many have recently begun to bemoan “reviewer fatigue.” As new journals come online and paper submissions to current journals are increasing, it is easy to assume that overwhelmed scholars decline requests to review or referee papers due to an excessive number of requests. This is largely a guess however and no one to my knowledge has yet empirically investigated why scholars decline a request to serve as an anonymous reviewer or referee. I’ve gotten some interesting data on this from reviewing requests for papers at the *American Political Science Review* (APSR). It turns out that reviewer fatigue is merely one possible factor in the scholars’ decision whether or not to agree to review/referee a paper, and only a small part of the APSR pool of potential reviewers have ever declined to agree to do so in my experience. Having a few more numbers, I had our administrative assistant do a survey of all recent requests for reviewers/review teams we made to find out what scholars say has led them to decline any such request. Turned out they remain quite willing to do this work — they just often decline doing so because of … life.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
